steps.description: Check that the ice maker is switched ON. There is typically a wire arm or a power switch on the ice maker itself — make sure it is in the ON/down position.
Verify the freezer temperature is at or below 15°F (−9°C). The ice maker will not cycle if the freezer is too warm. Adjust the temperature setting and wait 24 hours.
Inspect the water supply line behind the refrigerator. Ensure the saddle valve or shut-off valve is fully open and the line is not kinked or frozen.
Replace the water filter if it has been more than 6 months since the last change. A clogged filter can restrict water flow to the ice maker.
Check the ice maker fill tube (the small tube that delivers water into the ice tray) for ice blockage. If frozen, carefully thaw with a hair dryer on a low setting.
If none of the above resolves the issue, the ice maker assembly itself may be faulty and require replacement. Part PS11752778 is a common replacement for GE models.
Check that the refrigerator is plugged in and the circuit breaker is not tripped.
Verify the temperature controls are set correctly — refrigerator should be between 35°F and 38°F (1.7°C to 3.3°C).
Check the condenser coils (usually behind or underneath the unit). Dusty coils prevent heat dissipation. Vacuum them clean.
Listen for the evaporator fan motor running. If the freezer is cold but the refrigerator is warm, the evaporator fan or damper control may be faulty.
Inspect the door gaskets for tears or gaps that allow warm air to enter. Press a piece of paper against the gasket and close the door — if you can pull it out easily, the gasket needs replacing.
If the compressor is not running, check the temperature control thermostat (part WR55X10025 for GE models). A faulty thermostat will prevent the compressor from starting.
Remove and inspect the lower spray arm (part W10195682 for Whirlpool models). Check that all spray holes are open and unobstructed — use a toothpick to clear any clogged holes.
Remove and clean the dishwasher filter/screen at the bottom of the tub. A clogged filter is the most common cause of poor cleaning performance.
Check water temperature — the dishwasher needs water at 120°F (49°C) for effective cleaning. Run the hot water at your kitchen sink until hot before starting the dishwasher.
Verify detergent is fresh (clumped or old detergent does not dissolve properly) and the dispenser door opens during the wash cycle.
Ensure dishes are not blocking the spray arm rotation. Load dishes so spray can reach all surfaces and do not nest items together.
Clean the dishwasher filter and the drain area at the bottom of the tub. A blocked filter is the most common cause of drainage problems.
Check the drain hose (runs from the pump to the sink drain or garbage disposal) for kinks, clogs, or improper installation. The hose should have a high loop or air gap to prevent backflow.
If connected to a garbage disposal, verify the knockout plug inside the disposal inlet has been removed.
Run the garbage disposal to clear any blockage in the shared drain line.
If the drain pump motor is faulty, you will hear no pump sound during the drain cycle. The pump may require replacement.
